analogy: Think of "Fibre to Fabric" through the chapter's own opening images: Heat 3 Y ou know that woollen clothes are made from animal fibres. You also know that cotton clothes are made from plant fibres. We wear woollen clothes during winters when it is cold outside. We prefer to wear light coloured cotton clothes when it... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Fibre to Fabric is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that heat 3 Y ou know that woollen clothes are made from animal fibres.

The text explains that you also know that cotton clothes are made from plant fibres.

The text explains that we wear woollen clothes during winters when it is cold outside.

The text explains that we prefer to wear light coloured cotton clothes when it is hot.

The text explains that you might have wondered why particular types of clothes are suitable for a particular season.

The text explains that in summer, you feel hot even inside the house.

The text explains that how do we know whether an object is hot or cold?

The text explains that how do we find out how hot or cold an object is?

The text explains that in this chapter we shall try to seek answers to some of these questions.

The text explains that 3.1 H OT AND COLD In our day-to-day life, we come across a number of objects.

The text explains that some of them are hot We see that some objects are cold while some are hot.

The text explains that you also know that some objects are hotter than others while some are colder than others.

The text explains that how do we decide which object is hotter than the other?

The text explains that put cold water in container A and hot water in container Table 3.1: Hot and cold objects Object Cold/Cool Warm/Hot Ice cream  Spoon in a tea cup Fruit juice Handle of a frying pan Fig.

The text explains that 3.1 Feeling water in three containers (A) (B) (C) and some of them are cold.

The text explains that list some objects you use commonly in Table 3.1.

The text explains that a reliable measure of the hotness of an object is its temperature.

The text explains that temperature is measured by a device called thermometer.

The text explains that 3.2 M EASURING TEMPERATURE Have you seen a thermometer?

The text explains that recall that when you or someone else in your family had fever, the temperature was measured by a thermometer.

The text explains that the thermometer that measures our body temperature is called a clinical thermometer.

The text explains that 3.2 A clinical thermom- eter A clinical thermometer reads temperature from 35°C to 42°C.

The text explains that first, note the temperature difference indicated between the two bigger marks.

The text explains that the temperature should always be stated with its unit, °C.

The text explains that what did you record as your body temperature?

The text explains that the normal temperature of human body is 37°C.

The text explains that note that the temperature is stated with its unit.

The text explains that is the body temperature of every person 37°C?

The text explains that the temperature of every person may not be 37°C.

The text explains that actually, what we call normal temperature is the average body temperature of a large number of healthy persons.

The text explains that the clinical thermometer is designed to measure the temperature of human body only.

The text explains that the temperature of human body normally does not go below 35o C or above 42 o C.

The text explains that the maximum and minimum temperatures of the previous day, reported in weather reports, are measured by a thermometer called the m a x i m u m - m i n i m u m thermometer.

The text explains that cAUTION Do not use a clinical thermometer for measuring the temperature of any object other than the human body.

The text explains that look at it carefully and note the highest and the lowest temperature it can measure.

The text explains that 3.4 A laboratory thermometer 3.3 LABORATORY THERMOMETER How do we measure the temperature of other objects?

The text explains that he wanted to measure the temperature of hot milk using a clinical thermometer.

The text explains that wait till the mercury thread becomes steady and note the temperature.

The text explains that this means that the temperature must be read while the thermometer is in water.

The text explains that you may recall that while taking your own temperature, you have to take the thermometer out of your mouth to note the reading.

The text explains that 3.5)  bulb should be surrounded from all sides by the substance of which the temperature is to be measured.

The text explains that 3.5 Measuring temperature of water with a laboratory thermometer Boojho now understands why clinical thermometer cannot be used to measure high temperatures.

The text explains that but still wonders whether a laboratory thermometer can be used to measure his body temperature.

The text explains that this is the temperature of water at that time.

The text explains that compare the temperature of water recorded by each student in the class.

The text explains that in fact, Paheli asks: “Does it mean that heat will not be transferred if the temperature of two objects is the same?” transferred from the end nearest to the flame to the other end?

The text explains that the cold water from the sides moves down towards the source of heat.

The text explains that the air near the heat source gets hot and rises.

The text explains that the temperature of the object increases due to the absorbed part of the heat.

The text explains that measure the temperature of water in both the cans.

The text explains that do you find any difference in the temperatures?

The text explains that note the temperature of water after 10–15 minutes.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 3: Fibre to Fabric

prelims_traps: Do not treat 3.1 H OT AND COLD and HEAT 25 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Fibre to Fabric; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Fibre to Fabric through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 3.1 H OT AND COLD and HEAT 25 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
